Growth: Relating your ideas with others to create a respectful and productive discussion was one of the most important things learned. I think cooperation is heavily stress upon when talking about research and this class does a great job at simulating that environment
Lab4: Doing research during the pandemic made it feel like we were exploring new territory. I think being on the leading edge of research is a really interesting experience. It made me feel more secure of my knowledge on how to run a study. Even though we were restricted to online surveys, it still felt like we had full control over the direction of where we wanted this research to go

Growth: A few of the most important things I have learned is how to appropriately work well with others in various groups, prepare for whole-class discussions, and complete IRB and pre-registration forms. This class has taught me to learn how to split work to complement people's strengths but come back together as a group and help one another. Within writing the lab reports I was able to execute these skills I learned. By learning how to adequately prepare for large discussions I can now take this into other discussion-based classes or graduate school. Learning how to read a social psychology article in depth and then apply it to a discussion is amazing and a great skill I've learned. Finally, learning how to complete IRB and pre-registration forms has been the best thing ever! I have never completely filled out an IRB form so this class has helped me so much for any future labs or studies I may run. The pre-registration form is amazing in its self because I did not know that was a thing before this class and now I know more because I can actually fill one out.
Lab4: My experience has been great in conducting and conceptualizing lab 4. The pandemic is a current issue so to be able to do groundbreaking data collection is amazing and probably a once in a lifetime experience. I found it somewhat difficult to conceptualize a pandemic because it's not a common occurrence. However, being able to design my own study with a group and actually put it out for people to take was a lot of fun! Overall, I have learned that this experience is extremely valuable. The data and variables my group worked with can help so many people in the future and not just with pandemics. Conducting this research has helped me academically prepare for future lab studies because I have never used an online survey platform before this. Personally, I can learn from the data and make sure I do not make mistakes that any participants may have in their stigma of people.

Growth: The most important things I've learned is how to not only conduct a social psychological study, but more deeply analyze studies that have already been written. Learning the proper methodology and how it should be written up in an article has been invaluable. I am confident it will help me with my SMP. This class has also taught me to look more closely at academic articles. To be honest, before I never really looked at the statistics of a result, I just relied on its corresponding written statement to learn if it was significant or not. However, now I know that the statistics are vital to look at, especially the p value because that written statement can be misleading. I know that paying attention to the details of the study will be important moving forward.
Lab4: My group spent a good amount of time conceptualizing our variable for Lab 4. We had trouble finding a second independent variable to manipulate, so it took at lot of brainstorming and critical thinking. It was made harder by the fact that we had to find a way to manipulate the variable online since that was the only format available for our study. Additionally, the statistical analysis of our data was complicated by the fact that we did not have our normal access to the software needed for analysis. Communication and work for the group also had to be done virtually and different group members had wifi issues (through no fault of their own!) which was also not ideal. However, on the positive side, the pandemic offered a unique set of circumstances for research that could not otherwise be simulated. This lab has been a good way of gaining research experience and learning to work collaboratively with others.

Growth: The most important thing I have learned through this class is what makes a good paper and what doesn't. We read multiple papers, some good, some bad. The good ones all had one thing in common, they were very descriptive in everything they wrote. They told you exactly what they did, who did what, why it was important, what could be improved on, and what future research could do. It has improved my writing skills dramatically.
Lab4: Lab 4 was one of the hardest, but important lab report I feel like we have done. It was difficult because there was not a lot of past research directly about COVID-19, so we had to find past literature on things like the flu or SARS virus and connect it to what we were looking into. It was also hard to get a lot of participants due to everyone being stressed and not necessarily paying attention or having the motivation to participate in a study. However, I think one great thing about lab four was that its the most relevant and prevalent thing we have examined or studied thus far.

Growth: Honestly, some of the most important things I learned happened at the beginning of the semester. We talked about the prevalence of p-hacking, HARKing, and fraud in general in psychology and across several fields, and it shocked me. We read a paper that estimated that 100% of psychologists have committed p-hacking, which was astounding and really exemplified the gravity of the situation. We also learned that journals generally don't like publishing null results, and they prefer an "interesting" or "breathtaking" result instead of a real, scientific one that may be boring. This shaped the way I've read scientific articles since, and it's made me realize that there is a lot of corruption and fraud in academia because there is such a focus on simply getting published. Now, I can see those nuances in articles that I wasn't able to before, and I can critique them accordingly, so I am a far more critical reader and researcher now. I've always been a critical reader and observer, but I've improved greatly still because of this class. I have never had a psychology (or other) professor talk about the flaws of their field, so it was really refreshing. I think that this information coming from a younger professor helped as well because she was able to understand the past way of doing things and critiqued it from a more modern perspective. An older professor who has knowingly and eagerly engaged in these unethical behaviors might be more resistant to telling their students about these issues, but they are such important issues to learn about.
Lab4: I personally enjoyed running a study that was relevant to today instead of running something that doesn't seem to have very applicable or important implications. My study researched anti-Asian attitudes due to the Coronavirus (and we just finished collecting data), so it'll be interesting to see if college students at SMCM harbor these anti-Asian attitudes that have been perpetuated by our current government and ignorant public ideologies, even if that bias is implicit. These results have direct implications for how people are treated due to the coronavirus, so it is so important to gauge the reality of the pandemic we're going through by using empirical research instead of relying solely on politically charged rhetoric. The only thing that was "bad" about it was that it is a constant reminder of the pandemic and of people's awful racist attitudes, which can feel rather dismal. This has reassured me that I want to do research that is directly relevant to bettering the lives of people today because I found this kind of relevant research far more engaging.

Growth: This course helped me to understand what being a member of the social research community entails. I learned how to dive deep into literature of specific focus through research. I learned how to discuss complex methodologies with the authors themselves. I spent lots of time analyzing the strengths and weaknesses of empirical  writing and research design. I also learned the extensive creativity and thoughtful required from researchers to expand on and replicate findings. Generally, I feel I also learned how to engage in complicated and sensitive discussions with peers that were at times very objective and at other times highly subjective.
Lab4: Much of the time spent on the lab, especially in the beginning, was focused on developing a relevant research question and hypothesis. My group members and I wanted to examine patterns that we had observed on social media that would manifest into life-saving and real world behaviors (e.g., willing to engage in social distancing). When the class decided to focus our research on COVID-19, we were unaware of how serious the virus was and the encouragement of social distancing was relatively new. I remember the morning before I went to that class was the first time I had read about flattening the curve. The interesting aspect of this lab was that there were so many external/circumstantial variables we could not predict like the Stay-at-Home order and the long-term closings of colleges/universities. From a statistical standpoint, this lab is my first project attempting to examine interactions between variables. Personally, this is the first project with actual data collection where I feel there is potential for significant results.

Growth: The most important things I learned were how to analyze academic sources in a way to find both the positives and negatives of the study. I also found it improved my discussion skills in a large setting.
Lab4: It was weird conducting research on the pandemic while living in it, it felt like it almost seperated me from what was going on and made me look at others daily interactions in a way to referred to my project. It also definitely made me feel like I was doing research that was more applicable than most school projects.

Growth: The most important things I have learned are the challenges that come with doing fieldwork and actually conducting research. In lab 3, we learned how hard it can be to observe and conduct research in the field by actually doing it with. St. Mary's students. It was awkward at times, hard to remain inconspicuous, and came with more challenges that we were not expecting. In lab 4, we learned how hard it can be to get participants to respond to online surveys as well as how time consuming and tedious the IRB and preregistration processes are.
Lab4: It was very interesting conducting research on the pandemic while it was actually occurring. It was challenging at times because things changed. For example, social distancing laws became mandatory after we created our survey, which definitely influenced our study. It was also very interesting because our research felt important and not so hypothetical. I have learned a lot about how flexible you need to be while conducting research about a phenomenon that is rapidly changing and ongoing. It has also taken up a lot of my time which has been a bit challenging.

Growth: I have learned how to manage my time better, and I have learned how to think about papers from a different, more critical perspective. I have also learned that reaching out to others is not as scary as it once was for me. Through this semester, I have reached out to people who I never thought would reply, and I received support from people who I really thought would not be accepting.
Lab4: Lab 4 was very difficult for me, because I was facing so many issues with internet access, which I unfortunately needed to be able to participate and contribute to the lab. However, my group mates were so understanding and supportive regardless, and when I was able to contribute, they welcomed me which restored my confidence. The actual project was interesting, because when we thought of our idea, it was during a time when there were no stay-at-home restrictions, which changed just before the beginning of data collection, so we had to re-frame our idea a little bit. I think that communicating virtually through video conferencing allowed us to meet and discuss our project much more often than if we had only been able to meet in person. We met more often than I have ever met with a lab group before, just because it was more convenient to over the internet.

Growth: I think the most important things I've learned are related to reading journal articles critically. This is something I've been taught before but this class took it to the next level. Learning about p-hacking, HARKing, journals' bias against null hypotheses and unsupported hypotheses, the research demands of grad school, etc. has given me a lot of insight into how articles are written and how unreasonable standards in the community may be affecting research results. I think this is important context to have when looking at all scientific research.
Lab4: It was kind of strange to conduct research on a phenomenon as it was occurring. Usually lab research leans more towards replication, so it was cool to research something new. I don't know if there was anything bad about this research, everything went okay. Our results suggested that students aren't stigmatizing older people more, and that they are stigmatizing people not wearing masks and gloves. This is a lot better than the opposite which we thought would happen, and gives me hope that more people than not see the value of wearing protective gear. Academically this was a new and interesting experience. Personally, this gave me hope that at least some people are practicing social distancing as they should be.

Growth: The most important thing I learned was the mistakes that occur in social psychology and how they got out into the world. This was an interesting content in the course. I also like how my progress with writing lab reports is much better. I feel that I learned something new in every topic we covered, especially that we used empirical research to learn about it.
Lab4: I think this was good lab research. It is interesting to see how this pandemic is changing people's behaviors. I have learned based on my lab research that when a person sees another individual without a mask or protective gear, people stigmatize them more than when an individual does have the protective gear. However, I did not like that it was bout the pandemic. I am really tired of hearing people talk about it on the news or anywhere because it has impacted my personal life.

Growth: Being one of my first discussed based classes, this class was a new challenge for me which was intellectually stimulating. This helped me to better understand and dissect articles as well as begin to comprehend the flaws that can easily evolve around collecting data, analyzing data, formulating hypothesis, and bringing all information together to formulate an accurate and honest paper. This classes has forced me to speak my mind about ideas pertaining to the topic at hand and helped me to concisely formulate what I have to say in order for it to make sense to others, as public speaking is one of my weaknesses. With the amount of work and reading in this class it has helped me to time manage better and be more cognizant of the scientific articles I am reading. With the hypothesis it has helped me to continually engage with questions regarding to all areas of science in order to help learning.
Lab4: While this study was mostly initiated as conducting an online study prior to starting the experiment that aspect was not changed by the pandemic, in fact it helped shaped the hypothesis and concepts to be observed within the study. However, complications that arose with conducting the study during the pandemic typically were due to technological struggles such as ensuring everyone was able to meet at a certain time and the difficulties of working on an assignment or document all together while not being able to physically be together. Data collection and cleaning by using SPSS was particularly difficult, but not being physically with each other forced direct and supportive communication which was one of our group's strong points. This research was especially relevant due to the current pandemic situation and I believe it reflected some of my strengths and weaknesses in regard to my work and processes behind conducting my own work. This study, especially due to the pandemic challenged me and I now know of more areas in academia that I need to further educate myself with such as SPSS, but I also think I have a better understanding and appreciation of the extent that goes into conducting research.
